title-group.article-title: Meta-analysis of the efficacy of palivizumab versus nirsevimab at preventing medically attended respiratory syncytial virus infections in non-hospitalized preterm infants

contrib-group.aff: aScientific Services, Violicom Medical Limited, Aldermaston, UK
bNeonatology, McMaster University, Hamilton, Ontario, Canada

author-notes.corresp: CONTACT Barry Rodgers-Gray barry@violicom.co.uk Scientific Services, Violicom Medical Limited, Unit 1, Andromeda House, Calleva Park, Aldermaston, UK.

permissions.copyright-statement: © 2026 The Author(s). Published with license by Taylor & Francis Group, LLC.

license.license-p: This is an Open Access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial License (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/4.0/), which permits unrestricted non-commercial use, distribution, and reproduction in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited. The terms on which this article has been published allow the posting of the Accepted Manuscript in a repository by the author(s) or with their consent.

abstract.title: ABSTRACT
abstract.p: Palivizumab has >27 y of proven effectiveness in the prevention of severe respiratory syncytial virus (RSV) infection in high-risk infants. However, there remains limited data on its efficacy at preventing medically attended, non-hospitalized RSV infections (MARI). A systematic literature review was undertaken to identify randomized, placebo-controlled studies of prophylactic interventions against MARI and RSV-related hospitalization in healthy infants born ≤35 weeks’ gestational age (wGA). The findings informed a meta-analysis of three studies (N = 2,464) that found palivizumab significantly reduced MARI by 70.5% compared with placebo in 29–35 wGA infants, with broadly similar efficacy to nirsevimab.

fig.long-desc: We conducted a systematic literature review to identify studies assessing passive immunoprophylaxis with monoclonals vs placebo for the prevention of RSVH and/or MARI in preterm infants born less than or equal to 35 wGA. There were 234 records identified, and 161 records assessed for eligibility resulting in 3 relevant studies identified: IMpact-RSV (palivizumab vs placebo); MAKI (palivizumab vs placebo); Griffin et al: (nirsevimab vs placebo). A bayesian network meta-analysis assessed the effect of palivizumab on MARIas shown in the network diagram. Palivizumab significantly reduced MARI rate vs placebo by 70.5%. (Median ratio MARI rate palivizumab vs placebo.: 1:3.43, 95% CI:1.75 to 6.17). No significant difference in MARI rates between palivizumab and nirsevimab was observed. The median difference of MARI rate ratios for palivizumab vs placebo compared with nirsevimab vs placebo was 0.27 (95% CI: -2.20 to 2.37). Palivizumab reduced the risk of MARI in otherwise healthy premature infants, adding to its recognized benefit in the reduction of RSVH.
fig.alt-text: Systematic literature review and meta-analysis infographic on the efficacy of available passive immunoprophylaxis at preventing medically attended RSV infections.

funding-group.funding-statement: Financial support for this study was provided by AstraZeneca. All authors contributed to the development of the publication and maintained control over the final content.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Respiratory syncytial virus (RSV) is a leading cause of respiratory infections in young children and contributes to substantial morbidity and mortality, particularly in high-risk infants who are born preterm.1 Palivizumab prophylaxis has been proven effective at reducing the risk of RSV hospitalization (RSVH) in high-risk infants who are ≤35 weeks’ gestational age (wGA) and those with bronchopulmonary dysplasia (BPD) and hemodynamically significant congenital heart disease (HS-CHD), as evidenced in randomized controlled trials (RCTs)2,3 and over 27 y of real-world use.4 Despite the development of newer RSV preventives, including nirsevimab, clesrovimab and the maternal RSVpreF vaccine, uptake of these agents continues to be limited in many low- and middle-income countries,5 with palivizumab being the only available option (currently available in 93 countries worldwide; AstraZeneca, personal communication, March 10, 2026). It is increasingly recognized that medically attended RSV infection not requiring hospitalization (MARI) can make a substantial contribution to the acute burden of RSV illness.6,7 Although more recent studies of newer RSV preventive agents include MARI as a specific outcome,8 this was not the case for the earlier pivotal RCTs of palivizumab.2,3 This meta-analysis was primarily aimed to assess the efficacy of palivizumab in the prevention of MARI in healthy, preterm infants born at ≤35 wGA, including an assessment of its relative efficacy compared to nirsevimab.

sec.title: Identification of studies
sec.p: To support the meta-analysis, a systematic literature review was undertaken to identify randomized controlled studies (RCTs) of prophylactic interventions (monoclonals and maternal vaccine) versus placebo in the prevention of MARI and RSVH in infants ≤35 wGA without comorbidities (e.g. BPD, HS-CHD). A brief description of the systematic literature review is provided below with the full protocol registered in PROSPERO (ID: CRD42025638486; Supplementary Appendix Table 1).

Literature searches were conducted across PubMed, EMBASE, and the Cochrane Library databases, from January 1, 1998 to December 31, 2024 using the following search terms: RSV OR “respiratory syncytial virus” OR bronchiolitis OR “lower respiratory tract infection (LRTI)” OR “acute respiratory tract infection (ARTI)” OR “lower respiratory infection (LRI)” OR “acute respiratory infection (ARI)” OR “acute lower respiratory infection (ALRI)” OR human respiratory syncytial virus [MeSH/ESH]; (preterm OR prematur*) OR ((“29” OR “30” OR “31” OR “32” OR “33” OR “34” OR “35” OR “36”) AND (gestational age OR weeks’ gestational age OR wGA)); palivizumab OR Synagis OR nirsevimab OR Beyfortus OR motavizumab OR Numax OR suptavumab OR clesrovimab OR RSV vaccine OR Abrysvo OR prophylaxis OR immunoprophylaxis OR monoclonal antibod* OR mAb OR monoclonal antibodies [MeSH/ESH] (see Supplementary Appendix Table 1 for more details). Using a standardized template, two reviewers (BRG and NW) screened titles and abstracts and full-text articles to select and extract data from relevant RCTs meeting the Population, Intervention, Comparison, Outcomes, and Study (PICOS) inclusion criteria. Grey literature sources (e.g. conference proceedings) and bibliographies of included papers were also searched to identify any relevant studies not indexed in the database searches. Risk of bias was assessed using the Cochrane Collaboration Risk of Bias tool.9 All authors approved the final list of studies to be included in the meta-analysis.

sec.title: Statistical analysis
sec.p: A Bayesian network meta-analysis was undertaken linking available study data on RSVH and MARI rates in a logical block diagram versus placebo as the common comparator. Bayesian inferential output (10,000 Markov-Chain-Monte-Carlo [MCMC] iterations) was used to derive the median ratio of the MARI rate (i.e. the rate of non-hospitalized RSV infection) for palivizumab versus placebo. Using this ratio, the MARI rate in the palivizumab group was estimated from the base rate in the placebo group and the relative risk reduction (RRR) of prophylaxis calculated. Since palivizumab and nirsevimab target different sites on the RSV prefusion F protein (site II vs site Ø, respectively),10,11 and have differing half-lives (~20 d vs ~71 d),12,13 and preclinical activity against RSV has been reported to be >50 fold greater for the latter over the former,14 we explored whether these differences translate into a potential difference in efficacy between the two monoclonals. Assuming that the relative efficacy of RSV preventives against RSVH and MARI rates will run parallel and proportionately, palivizumab efficacy at preventing MARI was compared to that of nirsevimab by assessing the difference between the median ratios (versus placebo) in pairwise comparisons, with a probability of >0.05 indicating similar effectiveness. No heterogeneity tests across studies were undertaken as Bayesian meta-analysis accounts for such testing by enabling prior probability component variables to comply with their unique distributions through Gibbs sampling (MCMC) while generating posterior probabilities. WinBUGS version 1.4.3 was used for the Bayesian meta-analysis, with the validity of the results for the relative efficacy of palivizumab versus nirsevimab supported by non-stochastic linear manipulation of the same data in EXCEL 365.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: The systematic literature review assessed 234 citations, of which six research articles covering five RCTs met the eligibility criteria (Supplementary Appendix Table 2 & Supplementary Appendix Figure 1).2,8,15–20 Two of the studies were on palivizumab (IMpact and MAKI,2,15 reporting on RSVH and RSVH/MARI, respectively), two on nirsevimab (Griffin et al.8 and Domachowske et al.,17 both reporting RSVH/MARI), and one on suptavumab (Simoes et al.,18 RSVH/MARI). The risk of bias for all five RCTs was assessed as low. Two studies were subsequently excluded from the meta-analysis. One was excluded due to insufficient data (Domachowske et al.17 reported no RSVH or MARI cases in the placebo arm). The other study was excluded because suptavumab did not reduce overall RSVH/MARI rates (versus placebo) and its clinical development was halted.18 Since the three remaining studies spanned different gestational age ranges and follow-up periods (IMpact: ≤35 wGA, 150 d; MAKI: ≥33 to ≤35 wGA, median 10 months; Griffin et al.: ≥29 to ≤35 wGA, 150 d), the data from the IMpact trial used in the meta-analysis was aligned with Griffin et al. at 29–35 wGA.2,8,15 In total, the meta-analysis included data from 2,464 infants across three studies (Supplementary Appendix Figure 2).

The median ratio of the MARI rate for palivizumab versus placebo was 1:3.43, with the 95% credible interval not spanning zero (CrI, 1.75–6.17), indicating statistical significance (Figure 1(A)).19,20 This ratio translated into a 70.5% RRR in MARI for palivizumab over placebo (2.8% vs 9.5%, respectively). The difference between median ratios of the MARI rates for palivizumab versus placebo and nirsevimab versus placebo was 0.27, with the 95% CrI (−2.20–2.37) indicating no significant difference in efficacy between the two preventives (Figure 1(B)).19,20Figure 1.
Posterior probability distribution of medically attended RSV infections not requiring hospitalization. (A) Palivizumab vs placebo; (B) palivizumab vs placebo compared with nirsevimab vs placebo.The image shows two posterior probability distributions of non-hospitalized, medically-attended RSV infections. On the left hand side, image A shows the posterior proavability distribution for palivizumab versus placebo, whereas on the right hand side, image B shows the posterior probability distribution of palivizumab versus placebo compared with nirsevimab versus placebo. The vertical y-axis represents the sample frequency, and the horizontal x-axis represents the incidence ratio. Image A displays a right-skewed distribution curve. Below the graph, a table presents statistical measures: Mean 3.61, Standard deviation 1.41, Monte-Carlo error 0.01, Median 3.43 and 95% Credible interval ranging from 1.75 to 6.17. The image B displays the second distribution curve centered around 0. Below the graph, a table presents statistical measures: Mean 0.21, Standard deviation 1.41, Monte-Carlo error 0.01, Median 0.27 and 95% Credible interval ranging from minus 2.20 to 2.37.Posterior probability distributions of non-hospitalized, medicallyattended RSV infections.RSV, respiratory syncytial virus.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: RSVH typically accounts for the majority of direct medical costs associated with RSV in infants due to high individual case costs.6 However, it is increasingly recognized that the economic burden posed by MARI should also be considered, as, despite the individual case costs being lower, incidence rates may be twice as high as for RSVH.6,8 In a Canadian health economic assessment of palivizumab in infants born 32–35 wGA, MARI represented approximately 5% of hospital-related healthcare costs related to RSV infection,21 and this proportion would have been even greater, if the burden on primary care was also captured. The importance of the contribution of MARI to the burden of RSV infection is reflected in clinical trial endpoints assessing the efficacy of RSV preventives that have evolved over time from considering only hospitalization in the IMpact study2 to including the effect on both outcomes (alone and in combination) in recent trials.8,18 This meta-analysis found palivizumab prophylaxis to be associated with a statistically significant 70.5% RRR in MARI versus placebo, demonstrating broadly comparable efficacy to nirsevimab.19,20

The main limitations of the meta-analysis were that no MARI data were available for infants born <29 wGA and that the MAKI study included a narrower gestational age range of 33–35 weeks compared to the data reported in the IMpact and Griffin et al. studies.2,8,15,16 The study populations also varied in terms of geographical location and the time the studies were conducted: USA, Canada and UK from 1996 to 1997 (IMpact-RSV2,15); the Netherlands from 2008 to 2010 (MAKI16); and 23 countries from 2016 to 2017 (Griffin et al.8). An important aspect of the effectiveness of RSV preventives is their duration of protection, and this may extend for up to 12 months for nirsevimab.22 The duration of efficacy against MARI could not be assessed specifically in our analysis due to the limited number of studies available for inclusion. The follow-up period was 150 d in the IMpact (palivizumab) and Griffin et al. (nirsevimab) studies and a median of 10 months in the MAKI trial (palivizumab).2,8,15,16 Of note, limitations in the number and sample size of the studies included in the analyses are reflected in the credible intervals reported for the outputs and the strength of the conclusions that have been drawn from them. Our study is based upon a network meta-analysis in which the efficacy of each preventative – whether derived from either RSVH or MARI rates – is calculated as a probabilistic outcome based on Gibbs sampling and Bayesian inference, drawn directly from the patient numbers reported in each source publication. An analogous process was then utilized to combine the individual rates, around an element common to the source publications, to provide a probabilistically derived efficacy projection. It is intrinsic to the methodology that the stochastic spread of the output of such analyses (posterior probability) is directly affected by the size of the studies used as input (priors). Predicated on these results and in the absence of any head-to-head comparative data, we feel that it is reasonable to conclude that the efficacy at preventing MARI is broadly similar for palivizumab and nirsevimab. This conclusion is supported by the fact that the reported efficacy for palivizumab and nirsevimab at preventing RSVH in the registrational studies was 78% (in otherwise healthy infants born ≤35 wGA) and 78.4% (in otherwise healthy 29–35 wGA infants), respectively.2,8,15 Finally, we acknowledge that the definition of what constitutes MARI varies between studies, which complicates cross-trial comparisons. A clear and standardized definition for MARI (and RSVH) is needed that can be applied in diverse healthcare settings to support informed decision-making about their clinical- and cost-effective deployment.

MARI is now an essential clinical outcome employed to evaluate the overall burden of RSV and the effectiveness of emerging preventives. While palivizumab has well-established efficacy and effectiveness against RSVH in high-risk children, including preterm infants, data on its impact on MARI are more limited.4 This is the first meta-analysis on the efficacy of palivizumab in the prevention of MARI in otherwise healthy preterm infants, which lends further robustness to the preexisting evidence of its effectiveness in the reduction of RSVH.

ack.title: Acknowledgments
ack.p: The authors thank Oana Coban, PhD, of Violicom Medical Communications for editorial support.

bio.p: Barry Rodgers-Gray, BSc, MSc, PhD, has worked in medical education and communications for over 20 y. He first worked on RSV in 2003 and has since been involved in over 30 publications. Notable work includes being part of an international expert group that developed and validated the International Risk Scoring Tool for identifying infants born 32–35 weeks’ gestational age at greatest risk of RSV hospitalization. Barry was also one of the investigators on the EROS study that assessed the burden to parents of having a child hospitalized with RSV. Barry is a member of the PROUD Taskforce of 24 RSV global experts assembled to help identify solutions to the burden of RSV in low- and middle-income countries.

sec.title: Disclosure statement
sec.p: XCE and BP have received research funding and/or compensation as advisor/lecturer from AstraZeneca and/or Sanofi and/or Pfizer are outside the scope of this study. BRG, IK, NW and JF employer has received payment from AstraZeneca for work on various projects.

sec.title: Supplemental material
sec.p: Supplemental data for this article can be accessed online at https://doi.org/10.1080/21645515.2026.2652679
